Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic but I made it work. I'm 24 and working part-time to support myself, so I had to squeeze in study time wherever I could find it. I'd usually block out chunks on weekends and late nights when I could really focus. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps from the course materials. It wasn't the most organized system, but with 28 hours a week of studying across 16 courses, I had to be strategic about what got my attention.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying every evening after work. Other weeks, especially when bills were tight or I picked up extra shifts, everything got pushed to the last few days before deadlines. I tried to keep some regularity, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're doing this on your own without much financial cushion. The anxiety of falling behind actually pushed me to find pockets of time I didn't know I had.
Q3: Usually it was deadlines that got me to log in, I won't lie about that. But I also logged in when I felt that creeping anxiety about falling behind, which happened often. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post from a peer and that would pull me back in. The peer support aspect of this course actually surprised me, it made me want to check in more than I expected. When classmates were active, I felt more motivated to show up too.
Q4: It took a while, but eventually checking the LMS became something I did during my lunch breaks at work. I'd pull it up on my phone and at least scan what was new or what was coming up. The peer interactions helped make it feel less like a chore and more like checking in with people who understood what I was going through. Once I connected with a few other students who were also juggling a lot, logging in felt more natural. It wasn't automatic, but it became part of my rhythm.
Q5: The video lectures were huge for me because I'm such a visual learner. I watched almost 287 minutes of video content and I'd often pause to sketch out what was being explained. The discussion forums also turned out to be more valuable than I expected because hearing how other students approached problems gave me new perspectives. When peers broke down concepts in their own words, sometimes it clicked better than the official materials. Those interactions kept me going when I felt overwhelmed.
Q6: I'll admit I skipped a lot of the supplementary readings and some of the optional modules. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would directly help my assignments and exams. Some of the text-heavy resources just didn't stick for me the way visual content did. I also ignored some of the early introductory materials because I felt like I could figure things out as I went. Looking back, maybe I missed some foundational stuff, but I had to make choices.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The peer discussions were worth it because they reduced my anxiety about being the only one confused. If something felt like busywork or repetition of what I already understood, I'd skip it. Time is precious when you're stretched thin, so everything had to earn its place in my schedule. I also valued anything that gave me feedback quickly.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required work got done no matter what, even if I was exhausted or stressed. Optional stuff, I'd look at it and think 'that would be nice' but then something urgent would come up. The surprising thing was that some optional peer discussions ended up being really helpful when I did participate. But mentally, required and optional were in completely different categories for me. Required was survival, optional was extra.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good wifi, then study them on the bus or during slow moments at work. I filled notebooks with hand-drawn diagrams and visual summaries because that's how information sticks for me. I also used free YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't fully understand. My internet access isn't always reliable, so offline studying was essential, not optional.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be deep in my notes and diagrams, working through problems, but never touching the LMS. I'd print out materials early in the week and then study from paper for days. Sometimes I'd be explaining concepts to myself out loud, testing my understanding, and none of that shows up anywhere. My best learning often happened with a notebook and colored pens, creating visual maps of how concepts connected. The LMS only saw me when I submitted work or posted something.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download the slides, and then never go back to the LMS version. I'd work from those downloaded materials for weeks sometimes. My phone storage was full of PDFs and saved videos from the course. It was more practical given my situation, I couldn't always count on having internet when I needed to study. So one good download session would fuel a lot of offline learning.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I had a period where my work schedule got intense and I was barely sleeping, and my attendance dropped significantly during that time. The anxiety would spike and sometimes I'd freeze instead of engaging. Financial stress made it hard to concentrate even when I had time to study. But I kept pushing through because I've worked too hard to get here to give up now.
Q13: Definitely, all of those things affected me. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion made me avoid certain modules because I didn't want to face how lost I felt. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS got whatever scraps of attention I had left. There were weeks where I was running on fumes and my engagement was purely mechanical. But somehow I kept completing assignments, even if my process wasn't pretty.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while I was too tired to really process them. I'd be logged in, technically watching, but my mind was elsewhere worrying about rent or work. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, even though nothing was sinking in. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions. Real engagement required mental energy I didn't always have available.
Q15: I postponed the harder assignments that required more creative thinking because my brain needed to be in the right state for those. When I was stressed or anxious, I'd do the simpler tasks and avoid the challenging ones. Sometimes I knew something was important but the anxiety made it feel impossible to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, and sometimes tomorrow never came until the deadline forced my hand. It's a pattern I'm aware of but still struggle with.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity in the days before something was due, then go quiet for a while. My login patterns probably looked like a heartbeat monitor, spikes and flatlines. The pressure of deadlines actually helped me focus when my anxiety was manageable. But sometimes multiple deadlines would collide and I'd have to make tough choices about what got my full attention. That's probably why my assignment scores varied quite a bit.
Q17: Feedback was huge for my motivation. When I got positive feedback, I felt energized to keep going and engage more. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety, but once I processed it, it helped me focus my efforts better. I appreciated when instructors or peers gave specific suggestions because vague feedback left me more confused. The peer support in this course meant I got informal feedback too, which was surprisingly encouraging. Good feedback reminded me I was capable of this.
Q18: The structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan around my work schedule. But when the structure was too rigid or didn't account for students with complicated lives, it felt punishing. The peer support elements were structured in a way that actually worked for me, it wasn't forced but it was encouraged. I appreciated that the course allowed some flexibility in how we engaged with materials.
Q19: The visual materials and video content made engagement easier because they matched how I learn. The peer interaction components were surprisingly helpful for someone like me who might otherwise feel isolated. What made it harder was when there were too many small deadlines close together, it overwhelmed my already packed schedule. Also, some of the text-heavy resources weren't accessible to my learning style. More visual options throughout would have helped me engage more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things without feeling ready to commit to actual work. You might log in repeatedly, watch videos, read discussions, but the anxiety prevents you from actually producing anything. Or maybe they're struggling to understand the material so they keep reviewing without being able to complete assignments. It could also be someone whose life circumstances prevent them from having the uninterrupted time needed to write and submit work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS engagement was technically 'low' but I still earned a Merit because so much of my real learning happened offline. I'd download everything, study intensively from printed materials, then show up just to submit work and participate in key discussions. If you only looked at my login frequency, you'd think I was barely trying. But I was putting in 28 hours a week, just not all of it visible to the system. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on videos, and whether you meet deadlines. That's useful data. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually understanding or just clicking through. They can't see offline studying, which for students like me is where most learning happens. They also can't capture the emotional journey, the anxiety before logging in, the relief after submitting, the connections made with peers that happen outside the platform. Numbers tell part of the story, not the whole thing.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and grow. It's not just showing up, it's caring about whether I'm actually learning. For me, engagement includes the late nights with my notebooks, the moments when a concept finally clicks, the peer conversations that make me feel less alone in this. It's pushing through even when anxiety tells me to give up. True engagement is internal, it's about investment and effort, not just visible activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I've overcome to be here at 24, doing this without much financial support. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I manage every day or the resilience it takes to keep going when things get hard. It can't see the peer connections that genuinely surprised me and kept me motivated. My offline study habits, my visual learning strategies, my growth in confidence, none of that appears in the data. The numbers show I did okay, but they don't show what it cost me or what it meant to me to succeed.
